locked_facts: Standing wave formed by two identical waves travelling in opposite directions
Nodes: points of ZERO displacement always — fixed forever
Antinodes: points of MAXIMUM displacement — oscillate between +A and -A
Frequency of nth harmonic = n × fundamental frequency
Energy does not propagate in a standing wave
truth_statements: A standing wave is formed by the superposition of two identical waves travelling in opposite directions
At nodes, the displacement is always zero
At antinodes, the displacement varies between maximum positive and maximum negative values
The distance between two consecutive nodes equals half the wavelength
Standing waves do not transport energy

aha_moment.what_to_show: Node points stationary in red with ZERO label, antinode points oscillating maximally in green. Clear visual: nodes NEVER move, antinodes swing between +A and -A.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Red dots at nodes remain perfectly still. Green dots at antinodes oscillate maximally. The difference in amplitude across the wave is the key insight — NOT all points have the same amplitude.

simulation_states.label: Two travelling waves approaching
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Two identical waves (blue and orange) travelling in opposite directions on the same string, shown separately.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: These are two waves of the same amplitude, wavelength, and frequency, moving in opposite directions.

simulation_states.label: Waves superpose — standing wave forms
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Both waves overlap. The resultant standing wave pattern appears. Individual waves shown faintly behind.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: The superposition of two counter-propagating waves creates a standing wave. Notice: some points vibrate a lot, others don’t vibrate at all.

simulation_states.label: Nodes and antinodes revealed
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Standing wave with red dots at nodes (zero displacement always) and green dots at antinodes (maximum displacement). Envelope shown. λ/2 distance marked.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Nodes have ZERO displacement at all times. Antinodes swing between +A and -A. NOT all points vibrate with the same amplitude — this is the key difference from a travelling wave.

simulation_states.label: Interactive — change harmonic
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Slider to change harmonic number n. More nodes and antinodes appear as n increases. Distance between nodes = λ/2 is always maintained.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Higher harmonics have more nodes and antinodes. The fundamental (n=1) has nodes only at the ends. Each harmonic’s frequency = n × f₁.

trigger_keywords: standing wave mein sabka amplitude equal hai
common_misconceptions.student_belief: All points on a standing wave vibrate with the same amplitude
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: In a standing wave, different points have different amplitudes. Nodes always have zero amplitude, antinodes have maximum amplitude, and points in between have intermediate amplitudes.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show nodes marked in red (zero displacement) next to antinodes in green (maximum displacement). The amplitude varies with position — this is what makes it a STANDING wave.
